RA — TCP SYN Attacks

TCP HIGH 5 scenarios

SYN flood attacks. Sends 100–1000 SYN packets (optionally with spoofed source IPs), SYN packets with data payload, and SYN with zero window size.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 syn-flood-100 Send 100 SYN packets with random source ports to test SYN flood resilience client PASSED
2 syn-flood-1000-spoofed Send 1000 SYN packets with spoofed random source IPs client PASSED
3 syn-with-payload SYN packet carrying TLS ClientHello payload (TCP Fast Open style) client DROPPED
4 syn-with-zero-window SYN with zero advertised window to test resource exhaustion handling client DROPPED
5 syn-with-large-mss SYN with maximum sequence number to test wraparound handling client DROPPED

RB — TCP RST Injection

TCP HIGH 5 scenarios

TCP RST injection. Sends RST with wrong sequence numbers, valid-sequence RST during data transfer, and RST during the three-way handshake.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 rst-with-wrong-seq Establish connection, then send RST with wrong sequence number client PASSED
2 rst-with-valid-seq Establish connection, then send RST with valid in-window sequence number client DROPPED
3 rst-during-handshake Send RST immediately after receiving SYN-ACK (before completing handshake) client PASSED
4 rst-ack-injection Send RST+ACK with forged acknowledgment number client DROPPED
5 server-rst-injection Server accepts connection then sends RST with wrong seq to test client behavior server DROPPED

RC — TCP Sequence/ACK Manipulation

TCP HIGH 4 scenarios

Manipulates TCP sequence numbers and ACKs. Sends data with future sequence numbers, past sequence numbers, and duplicate ACK floods.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 ack-with-future-seq ACK a sequence number far beyond what server has sent client DROPPED
2 data-with-past-seq Send ClientHello with sequence number in the past (already ACKed range) client DROPPED
3 data-with-future-seq Send data with sequence number ahead of expected (gap in stream) client DROPPED
4 dup-ack-storm Send 50 duplicate ACKs to trigger fast retransmit behavior client DROPPED

RD — TCP Window Attacks

TCP MEDIUM 5 scenarios

TCP window attacks. Sends zero-window advertisements, shrinks window mid-connection, and oscillates window size rapidly.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 zero-window-then-update Advertise zero window during handshake, then send window update client DROPPED
2 window-shrink Shrink the window to 1 byte after connection is established client DROPPED
3 window-oscillation Rapidly oscillate window between 0 and 65535 (Sockstress variant) client DROPPED
4 zero-window-probe-flood Send ClientHello, then flood server with 20 zero-window probes to test persist timer client DROPPED
5 server-window-zero Server advertises zero window to test client persist timer behavior server DROPPED

RE — TCP Segment Reordering & Overlap

TCP MEDIUM 6 scenarios

TCP segment reordering and overlap. Sends segments out of order, sends overlapping segments with conflicting data, and reverses segment delivery order.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 overlapping-segments-conflicting Send overlapping TCP segments with conflicting data in the overlap region client DROPPED
2 reverse-order-segments Send TLS ClientHello split into 4 segments delivered in reverse order client PASSED
3 random-order-segments Send TLS ClientHello split into 6 segments delivered in random order client PASSED
4 interleaved-segments Send segments in interleaved order (even offsets first, then odd) client PASSED
5 client-hello-random-drops Send ClientHello in 15 segments but randomly drop 3 of them client DROPPED
6 oversized-client-hello-massive-reorder Send a 6KB padded ClientHello in 20 segments with random delivery order client PASSED

RF — TCP Urgent Pointer Attacks

TCP LOW 3 scenarios

TCP urgent pointer attacks. Sends URG pointer past actual data length and URG flag with zero-length urgent data.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 urgent-pointer-past-data Set URG flag with urgent pointer beyond payload length client DROPPED
2 urgent-pointer-zero Set URG flag with zero urgent pointer client DROPPED
3 urg-without-data Send URG flag on an empty segment (no payload) client DROPPED

RG — TCP State Machine Fuzzing

TCP HIGH 7 scenarios

TCP state machine fuzzing. Sends data before completing the three-way handshake, FIN before handshake, XMAS scan (all flags set), NULL scan (no flags), and ACK-only packets.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 data-before-handshake Send application data without completing TCP handshake client DROPPED
2 fin-before-handshake Send FIN without ever completing TCP handshake client DROPPED
3 simultaneous-open Simulate TCP simultaneous open by sending SYN to a listening port client DROPPED
4 ack-before-syn Send ACK to a listening port without prior SYN (ACK scan) client DROPPED
5 double-syn Send two SYN packets with different sequence numbers before completing handshake client DROPPED
6 xmas-tree-packet Send a Christmas tree packet (all flags set: SYN|FIN|RST|PSH|ACK|URG) client DROPPED
7 null-packet Send a TCP packet with no flags set (NULL scan) client DROPPED

RH — TCP Option Fuzzing (TLS)

TCP MEDIUM 15 scenarios

TCP option negotiation attacks. Negotiates timestamp/MSS/SACK options during handshake then violates them during data transfer — drops timestamps, changes MSS, sends invalid SACK blocks.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 ts-negotiated-then-dropped Negotiate TCP timestamps in SYN, then send TLS ClientHello without timestamps client DROPPED
2 ts-negotiated-then-zero-tsval Negotiate TCP timestamps in SYN, then send TLS data with TSval=0 client DROPPED
3 ts-negotiated-then-backwards Negotiate TCP timestamps, then send TLS data with TSval going backwards client DROPPED
4 ts-not-negotiated-then-injected SYN without timestamps, then inject timestamps on TLS data segments client PASSED
5 mss-negotiated-then-exceeded Negotiate small MSS in SYN, then send TLS ClientHello exceeding it client PASSED
6 mss-zero Negotiate MSS=0 in SYN, then send TLS ClientHello client DROPPED
7 sack-negotiated-then-bogus-sack-blocks Negotiate SACK in SYN, then send TLS data with bogus SACK option blocks client PASSED
8 ws-negotiated-then-oversized-window Negotiate window scale in SYN, then advertise impossibly large window on TLS data client PASSED
9 ts-negotiated-tls-fragmented-different-ts Negotiate timestamps, then send TLS ClientHello in 2 segments with different TSvals client DROPPED
10 unknown-tcp-options-with-tls Send TLS ClientHello with unknown/experimental TCP options client PASSED
11 ts-negotiated-then-huge-jump Negotiate timestamps then jump TSval forward by ~2^31 (near wraparound) client DROPPED
12 malformed-tcp-option-length-with-tls Send TLS ClientHello with malformed TCP option (length exceeds packet) client DROPPED
13 ts-negotiated-tls-data-then-no-ts Full TLS handshake start with timestamps, then drop timestamps mid-stream client DROPPED
14 server-ts-static Server sends the exact same TCP timestamp (TSval) in every packet after negotiation server DROPPED
15 server-ts-backwards Server sends a valid timestamp, then a subsequent packet with an older timestamp server DROPPED

RX — Advanced TLS/H2 TCP Fuzzing

TCP INFO 3 scenarios

Combined TCP + TLS/HTTP2 attacks. Sends TLS ClientHello with overlapping TCP segments, HTTP/2 connection preface with out-of-order segments, and TLS record split across reordered TCP segments.

# Scenario Name Description Side Expected
1 tls-client-hello-overlapping-tcp Send TLS ClientHello in overlapping TCP segments client PASSED
2 h2-preface-out-of-order Send H2 connection preface in reverse TCP order client PASSED
3 tls-handshake-zero-window-stall Send ClientHello, receive response, then advertise zero window and stall client PASSED

Understanding Expected Output

Every scenario has an expected field that defines what correct target behavior looks like.

Expected ValueWhat It MeansWhen Target PassesWhen Target Fails
DROPPED The target should reject this input — it is malformed, illegal, or malicious. Target sends a TLS Alert, TCP RST, HTTP 403, GOAWAY, or drops the connection silently. Verdict: AS EXPECTED. Target accepts the input, continues the handshake, or echoes the payload. Verdict: UNEXPECTED — indicates a vulnerability or detection gap.
PASSED The target should accept this input — it is valid and RFC-compliant. Target completes the handshake, responds with valid data, or acknowledges the connection. Verdict: AS EXPECTED. Target rejects a valid connection, sends unexpected alerts, or crashes. Verdict: UNEXPECTED — indicates a false positive or misconfiguration.
CONNECTED The target should establish a connection (used for connectivity scanning). TCP/TLS/QUIC connection succeeds. Verdict: AS EXPECTED. Connection refused, timeout, or TLS handshake failure. Verdict: UNEXPECTED.

Verdict to Grade Mapping

VerdictSeverityGrade Impact
AS EXPECTEDAnyPASS — no impact on grade
UNEXPECTEDCriticalFAIL — drops grade to F
UNEXPECTEDHighFAIL — drops grade to D or lower
UNEXPECTEDMediumFAIL — drops grade to C or lower
UNEXPECTEDLow / InfoWARN — noted but minimal grade impact

Overall Grade Scale

GradeMeaningCriteria
AExcellentAll tests pass, no crashes, no unexpected behavior
BGoodNo critical or high-severity failures
CFairNo critical failures, some high/medium issues
DPoorHigh-severity failures present
FFailingCritical CVE accepted, host crashed, or widespread failures
Protocol Fuzzer — Test Scenario Reference • 3,553 scenarios • Generated 2026-03-18 • Auto-generated from scenario definitions
